label: LLM-as-judge & automated critique

node.label: LLM-as-judge & automated critique
node.definition: LLM-as-judge uses a model to score, compare, critique, or classify outputs under a rubric, often calibrated against human labels and bias checks.
node.why_it_matters: LLM-as-judge methods can scale rubric-based scoring and critique, but they still need calibration against human judgments and checks for bias or instability.
examples: pairwise answer comparison by a judge model
automated labeling followed by human calibration
limitations: Judge models can inherit model biases, favor familiar style over correctness, and disagree with humans when prompts, rubrics, or domains shift.

dependencies.rationale: LLM-as-judge methods are commonly calibrated, validated, or compared against human evaluation and preference studies.
LLM-as-judge evaluations commonly use benchmark tasks, rubrics, or task metrics to structure judge prompts and compare outputs.
Most current LLM-as-judge systems use transformer language models as the judge or critique model.
